title: Agentic Legal Platform — System Design
description: Design the platform that lets legal agents do real work — contract review and redlining, due-diligence at scale, and cited research memos — where the irreversible action behind the gate is a finalized or filed work product, with malpractice and privilege on the line. It builds on Agentic Platform Foundations (retrieval/grounding, eval, confidentiality) and, like the Finance vertical, leans deterministic: retrieve from authoritative sources → draft → verify every citation → attorney review. Its crux is a citation-verification gate (existence, currency, and relevance — a real-but-overruled citation is worse than a fabricated one) plus a mandatory attorney-in-the-loop, because hallucination is a uniquely dangerous, first-class risk.

nodes.label: Supervising Attorney
nodes.description: Licensed attorney who must review and approve before any work product is finalized or filed.

nodes.label: Matter Documents
nodes.description: Client matter inputs: contracts, due-diligence sets, the drafting request — treated as untrusted, tenant- and matter-scoped.

nodes.label: Legal API
nodes.description: Authenticates the matter, enforces tenant isolation, routes to the pipeline.

nodes.label: Research / Draft Pipeline
nodes.description: Deterministic DAG: retrieve -> draft -> verify -> attorney review. Not an open loop.

nodes.label: Draft Agent
nodes.description: LLM inside a bounded node: drafts memos/redlines grounded in retrieved sources, with citations.

nodes.label: Inference Backend
nodes.description: Shared LLM serving tier (from the Foundations platform), under no-train terms.

nodes.label: Authoritative Source Index
nodes.description: Grounding corpus: Westlaw / Practical Law / KeyCite and statutes — the citable, authoritative law.

nodes.label: Document Mgmt System
nodes.description: iManage/NetDocuments matter docs, preserving access controls and ethical walls.

nodes.label: Citation Verifier
nodes.description: Deterministic check of every citation: existence, currency (not overruled, via KeyCite), and relevance to the proposition.

nodes.label: Draft Work Product
nodes.description: Holds a draft through its whole lifecycle by status — unverified intermediate, verified awaiting review, finalized work product — never auto-filed.

nodes.label: Provenance Store
nodes.description: Maps every finding/sentence to the source span it came from (audit-defensible).

nodes.label: Confidentiality & Policy
nodes.description: Tenant isolation, ethical walls, zero-data-retention/no-train enforcement, document screening.

nodes.label: Identity & Token Broker
nodes.description: Scoped, delegated access to the matter; ties approval to a licensed attorney principal.

nodes.label: Audit Record
nodes.description: Append-only record: sources retrieved + versions, citations verified, attorney approval, event IDs.

nodes.label: Work Queue
nodes.description: Levels intake and carries due-diligence batches (tabular extract over many docs).

nodes.label: Observability & Eval
nodes.description: Trajectories, citation-error and hallucination rates, eval vs attorney ground truth.

functional: Review contracts against a playbook and produce redlines with rationale.
Run due-diligence review at scale (tabular extraction across thousands of documents).
Draft research memos and documents grounded in authoritative legal sources, with citations.
Verify every citation before any draft is surfaced to an attorney.
Require attorney review and approval before any work product is finalized or filed.
nonFunctional: Hallucination is a first-class risk: a wrong, overruled, or off-point citation must be caught, not surfaced.
Every finding is traceable to a source span (audit-defensible provenance).
Confidentiality and isolation: zero-data-retention, no-train, tenant segregation, ethical walls, SOC 2.
Grounding is over authoritative law (Westlaw/Practical Law/KeyCite) and matter docs, not the open web.
Deterministic control flow: retrieve → draft → verify → review, not an open autonomous loop.

capacity.label: Interactive memos
capacity.note: Memo/contract drafting is interactive: target p95 first-draft latency < 90s, so ~16k citation verifications/day on the interactive path alone.

capacity.label: Diligence batches
capacity.note: Each doc ~30 pages × ~700 tokens/page; a 10k-doc batch is ~200M input tokens. Offline SLA in hours, not seconds — resumable, checkpointed.

capacity.label: Citation verification
capacity.note: 1–2 KeyCite/Westlaw calls per citation; with provider rate limits this is the dominant external bottleneck — cached per (citation, version), batched, and circuit-broken.

capacity.label: Peak concurrency
capacity.note: Per-matter concurrency cap keeps one large diligence batch from starving interactive memos; a global inference/token budget caps spend. Excess work waits in the queue (DLQ on repeated failure).

capacity.label: Autonomy
capacity.note: Agents produce pending drafts; a licensed attorney finalizes — nothing auto-files.

api.description: Asynchronously enqueue a draft work product (grounded, cited, provenance-tracked) for attorney review — it does not file. Workflow-aware and idempotent.

Poll the split lifecycle. jobStatus walks queued → drafting → verifying → (correcting →) awaiting_review (or verification_blocked / human_exception on terminal failure); reviewStatus is the attorney decision once a human is involved. Diligence batches also report per-document progress and DLQ count.

A licensed attorney drives the review state machine; finalize/file is the irreversible action and is bound to the attorney principal. request_changes sends the draft back through the correction loop (jobStatus → correcting → verifying → awaiting_review). override_cite is allowed only if policy permits and is audited.
Fetch the provenance map: each finding/sentence linked to the source span it came from, plus citation-verification results.

dataModel.name: matters
dataModel.note: Tenant- and matter-scoped boundary: the ACL/ethical-wall context every retrieval, draft, and approval is checked against.

dataModel.name: documents
dataModel.note: Matter documents (DMS) and corpus sources, versioned so source spans stay stable.

dataModel.name: source_corpora
dataModel.note: First-class grounding sources with version pinning: an authoritative-law track (Westlaw/Practical Law/KeyCite, publisher snapshots) vs a client-matter track (DMS) that fail and refresh differently.

fields.name: freshness_sla
fields.type: interval (how stale a cached lookup may be)

dataModel.name: drafts
dataModel.note: A work product moving through job then review state — split so the async pipeline lifecycle and the attorney decision don't collide; never auto-filed.

dataModel.name: citations
dataModel.note: Each citation in a draft, the proposition it supports, and the latest verification decision.

dataModel.name: citation_verifications
dataModel.note: One row per verification attempt — the reviewable evidence behind a citation decision (relevance is a score, not a boolean).

dataModel.name: provider_calls
dataModel.note: One observable, auditable attempt against an external provider (KeyCite/Westlaw lookup or inference) — the unit that makes rate limits, caching, retries, freshness, and spend concrete.

fields.name: request_hash
fields.type: varchar (cache key: op + citation + source version)

fields.name: cost
fields.type: numeric (metered units / tokens, against per-matter budget)

dataModel.name: playbooks
dataModel.note: A firm's contract-review standard: an ordered set of rules a contract is checked against, version-pinned so a returned contract reuses the same rule set.

dataModel.name: playbook_rules
dataModel.note: One reviewable position in a playbook — the parent every contract-review redline ties to via rule_id.

dataModel.name: extraction_schemas
dataModel.note: The tabular schema a diligence batch fans across documents — the parent of every diligence_finding's schema_field.

fields.name: fields
fields.type: jsonb (column name -> type, required, confidence threshold)

dataModel.name: diligence_batches
dataModel.note: A resumable due-diligence run: an extraction schema fanned across many documents, checkpointed so per-document failure doesn't fail the batch.

dataModel.name: diligence_documents
dataModel.note: Per-document batch status: the unit of checkpointing, retry, and poison-document DLQ inside a diligence batch.

dataModel.name: redlines
dataModel.note: Contract-review output: a suggested edit tied to a playbook rule and a clause span, with rationale.

dataModel.name: diligence_findings
dataModel.note: Due-diligence output: one extracted cell per document × schema field, with confidence and a traced source span.

dataModel.name: review_events
dataModel.note: The attorney review workflow: assignment, returns, approvals, overrides — durable and ordered.

dataModel.name: provenance
dataModel.note: Maps a finding/sentence to the exact source span and version it came from.

dataModel.name: audit_events
dataModel.note: Append-only, immutable, retention-controlled: sources retrieved + versions, verification results, approvals, overrides.

technologyChoices.concern: API edge, matter intake, and tenant quotas
tradeoff: The edge should authenticate the attorney or firm application, enforce matter-level request shape, apply tenant quotas, and reject obvious abuse before work enters the legal pipeline.
Self-hosted gateways give exact control over custom matter headers, streaming uploads, and quota dimensions; managed gateways reduce TLS, WAF, and identity integration work.
Coarse quotas belong at the edge, but per-matter token budgets and attorney-review admission still belong in the application pipeline.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed edge with WAF, identity integration, and quota controls removes most custom reverse-proxy code, but not the matter-aware budget and ethical-wall checks.

technologyChoices.concern: Authoritative law retrieval and source index
tradeoff: The legal authority provider remains the source of truth; the platform's index is a scoped, version-pinned retrieval cache, not a substitute for KeyCite or publisher metadata.
Search engines are strong for lexical and metadata retrieval over statutes, cases, and practice notes; vector indexes help recall semantically similar authority but must still return citable spans.
Managed search and RAG services reduce index operations, but legal freshness, source-version pinning, and provider rate limits still need explicit cache keys and invalidation policy.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed search stack with built-in vector retrieval can remove custom ANN/index-serving code, but it does not remove the citation-verification gate.

technologyChoices.concern: Matter DMS ingestion, OCR, and document quarantine
tradeoff: A self-hosted intake path is easier to keep inside the firm's network and lets you quarantine untrusted documents before OCR, parsing, and indexing.
Managed OCR and classification services reduce parser maintenance for PDFs, scans, and tables, but they expand the set of services that must meet the firm's confidentiality terms.
Document ingestion must preserve the DMS access model: matter, tenant, jurisdiction, ethical wall, version, and retention policy travel with every extracted span.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed OCR plus object storage eventing removes most custom PDF worker scheduling, but not quarantine, access propagation, or provenance capture.

technologyChoices.concern: Deterministic workflow orchestration and correction loops
tradeoff: Workflow engines make retrieve -> draft -> verify -> review visible, retryable, and replayable, which is the core difference from an open autonomous loop.
Temporal-like runtimes suit long-running correction loops and attorney callbacks; cloud workflow services reduce operational load but can make complex business logic awkward in YAML or function glue.
A database-backed state machine is simpler to audit at small scale, but it usually grows custom retry, timer, and compensation machinery.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A durable workflow engine with timers, retries, callbacks, and execution history removes most hand-built correction-loop tables and retry daemons.

technologyChoices.concern: LLM inference, model gateway, and prompt caching
tradeoff: A model gateway centralizes model routing, prompt policy, no-train/no-retention configuration, token budgets, and fallback behavior for draft and relevance-scoring calls.
Self-hosting gives stronger data-boundary control and model portability, but shifts capacity planning, GPU operations, and safety filtering onto the platform team.
Managed model services reduce serving operations and add enterprise controls, but legal workloads still need explicit prompt redaction, request logging policy, and per-matter spend caps.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed inference platform with enterprise data controls and model routing removes most GPU-serving operations, but not the deterministic verification and attorney gates.

technologyChoices.concern: Citation verification adapter, cache, and circuit breakers
tradeoff: Citation verification is dominated by external authority-provider rate limits and freshness; cache keys must include citation, provider, operation, jurisdiction, and source version.
A cache is safe only for the freshness window promised by the legal source; stale good-law status is a correctness bug, not just a latency issue.
Circuit breakers protect the pipeline from provider outages, but the degraded mode is to block or escalate the draft, not to skip verification.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed key-value cache with TTLs plus durable workflow retries removes most custom provider-throttle and retry bookkeeping.

technologyChoices.concern: Draft work-product and review state store
tradeoff: Relational storage is the clearest fit for drafts, citations, review events, idempotency keys, and attorney approval because the invariants are cross-row and audit-facing.
Distributed SQL helps if a global firm needs multi-region writes, but the added latency and schema-change constraints are hard to justify before clear scale pressure.
Document or key-value stores can hold flexible work-product payloads, but review-state transitions and citation joins usually move back to relational tables.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed relational database removes most backup, failover, and patching work for work-product state, but not schema discipline around draft and review lifecycle transitions.

technologyChoices.concern: Due-diligence batch queue, fair scheduling, and token budgets
tradeoff: Large diligence batches need queue-based load leveling so a 10k-document review does not starve interactive memo drafting.
Logs and streams are useful when batch events need replay and multiple consumers; queues are simpler for per-document retries, poison documents, and DLQs.
The scheduler must allocate by matter, tenant, and token budget, not only by global worker availability.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed queues with DLQs and autoscaling consumers remove most broker operations, but not the fairness policy that protects interactive legal work.

technologyChoices.concern: Provenance graph and immutable audit record
tradeoff: Provenance is queryable state: finding -> source span -> source version -> draft sentence. Audit is append-only evidence: retrieval, verification, approval, and override events.
Relational or search stores make provenance explorable for attorneys; immutable object retention or ledger-style append logs make audit records harder to tamper with.
Analytics stores help evaluate citation-error and hallucination rates, but must not become the only copy of legally required audit evidence.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Object storage with retention locks and managed audit logging reduces the need for a custom WORM archive, as long as the application still writes complete event payloads.

technologyChoices.concern: Identity delegation, ethical walls, and approval policy
tradeoff: Legal approval needs delegation, not impersonation: the system can act on a matter only within scoped authority, and finalization binds to a licensed attorney principal.
Relationship-based authorization helps model ethical walls and matter teams; policy engines help encode approval rules, override rules, and jurisdiction constraints.
Secrets and token brokers must keep provider credentials out of prompts, logs, and worker environments that do not need them.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed identity and policy services remove much of the custom token-broker surface, but the legal domain model for matter access and attorney approval remains application-owned.

technologyChoices.concern: Evaluation harness, trajectory telemetry, and quality gates
tradeoff: Tracing captures the path a draft took; eval harnesses compare outputs to attorney ground truth and track citation-error, hallucination, override, and correction-loop rates.
Operational telemetry answers whether the pipeline is healthy; evaluation telemetry answers whether the legal work is trustworthy.
A promotion gate should fail a model or prompt release on legal-quality regressions, not merely display a dashboard after deployment.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed observability reduces collector and dashboard operations, but quality gates still need legal-domain datasets, labels, and release policy.

technologyChoices.concern: Confidential storage, retention, and sensitive-data controls
tradeoff: Legal data needs different retention classes for prompts, retrieved snippets, model outputs, eval traces, audit events, and finalized work product.
Encryption and secret custody are table stakes; the harder choice is where redaction, legal hold, tenant deletion, and immutable audit retention are allowed to conflict.
Managed discovery and DLP services help find sensitive content, but they must run under the same matter boundary and no-train/no-retention constraints as the pipeline.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed key custody, secret storage, and sensitive-data discovery remove many platform primitives, but not the retention policy matrix the application must enforce.

patterns.name: Workflow vs agent (deterministic pipeline vs probabilistic loop)
patterns.what: An authored DAG (retrieve -> draft -> verify -> review), not an open autonomous loop.
patterns.whenToUse: When a wrong citation is a malpractice-grade side effect to guard before surfacing.

patterns.name: Human-in-the-loop approval (gate)
patterns.what: A licensed attorney reviews and approves before any work product is finalized or filed.
patterns.whenToUse: Before any legal work product leaves the platform.

patterns.name: Bounded autonomy (agent + deterministic gate)
patterns.what: Agents draft freely but a deterministic verify gate and attorney review fence the irreversible action.
patterns.whenToUse: Draft-only autonomy with hard verification and review boundaries.

patterns.name: LLM-as-judge / trajectory evaluation
patterns.what: Verify citations and evaluate drafts against attorney ground truth; track hallucination and citation-error rates.
patterns.whenToUse: Measuring and gating the pipeline where hallucination is the core risk.

patterns.name: Memory: short-term vs long-term (semantic/episodic/procedural)
patterns.what: Retrieve from authoritative law and matter docs as grounding, scoped per tenant.
patterns.whenToUse: Grounding every draft in citable sources, not model memory.

patterns.name: Prompt-injection defense (CaMeL / Dual-LLM / Plan-then-Execute)
patterns.what: Treat matter documents as untrusted; partition them from tool authority and from other tenants.
patterns.whenToUse: Documents and opposing-party text are attacker-reachable.

patterns.name: Delegation not impersonation (OAuth token exchange, act claim)
patterns.what: Scoped, delegated matter access; approval is tied to a licensed attorney principal.
patterns.whenToUse: Carrying matter authority and binding approval to a licensed user.

patterns.name: Queue-based load leveling; admission control
patterns.what: Level intake and fan due-diligence batches across thousands of documents.

steps.title: 1. Naive: An Agent That Drafts From Its Own Knowledge
description: Start with the tempting demo: an agent answers a legal question or drafts a memo straight from the model, citing cases from memory.

In law, this is the malpractice version. Models hallucinate citations — and worse than a fabricated case is a real but overruled one that reads as good law. There is no grounding in authoritative sources, no citation check, no provenance, no attorney review, and no confidentiality story for client data sent to a model that might train on it.
The rest of the interview turns this into a grounded, verified, attorney-gated pipeline.

view.caption: An agent drafts from model memory and cites from memory — the hallucinated-citation malpractice baseline.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why is a confidently cited but unverified draft more dangerous in law than in most domains?
patterns: Workflow vs agent (deterministic pipeline vs probabilistic loop)
whyNow: The naive draft makes the hallucination and verification requirements concrete.

recap.after: An agent can produce a plausible legal draft.
recap.newRisk: Citations may be fabricated or overruled, nothing is grounded or verified, and client data isn't protected.

traps.trap: Trusting a fluent draft because its citations look real.
traps.why: A real-but-overruled citation reads as good law; fluency is not correctness, and the model can't tell.
traps.instead: Ground in authoritative sources and verify every citation before an attorney sees it.

steps.title: 2. Ground in Authoritative Law, Not Model Memory
description: The grounding corpus for this vertical is authoritative legal sources — Westlaw / Practical Law / KeyCite and statutes — plus the client's matter documents in the DMS. The agent must draft from retrieved, citable sources, not from the model's recollection.

Confidentiality is not a later hardening layer — it constrains retrieval from the very first read. Every retrieval is checked against the matter's access boundary: tenant, matter, jurisdiction, document version, and the firm's ethical walls. The Guardrail/access-policy service sits on the grounding path (not only in the later trust step), so a document the requesting attorney cannot see is never retrieved, and matter documents are treated as untrusted input — partitioned from other tenants and from tool authority. Sources are versioned at ingestion so a cited span stays stable.

view.caption: The agent grounds drafts in retrieved authoritative law and matter docs, scoped per tenant with ethical walls preserved.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why ground in Westlaw/KeyCite and the DMS rather than the open web or the model's own knowledge?

concepts.definition: Retrieving from curated legal databases and matter docs so every assertion can cite a real, current source.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is the only way to make citations checkable; ungrounded drafting is unverifiable by construction.
concepts.example: A proposition is drafted from a retrieved Westlaw headnote, with the case and its KeyCite status attached.

concepts.definition: Every retrieval is scoped to the requester's tenant, matter, and jurisdiction and screened against firm ethical walls before any document is returned.
concepts.whyItMatters: In a legal platform, data isolation changes which documents can even be retrieved and who can approve — it cannot be bolted on later.
concepts.example: An attorney walled off a matter retrieves nothing from it, even though the document physically exists in the DMS.

whyNow: Grounding is the corpus that makes verification possible; everything later assumes citable sources.

deepDives.title: Two source tracks with different lifecycles
points: The authoritative-law track (Westlaw/Practical Law/KeyCite, statutes) is shared across tenants, publisher-versioned, and refreshed on a freshness SLA — its failure mode is staleness (an overruled case the cache hasn't caught).

The client-matter track (DMS) is tenant- and matter-scoped, access-controlled by ethical walls, and versioned per document — its failure mode is leakage (returning a document the requester may not see).
Modeling them as separate source_corpora makes version pinning, refresh, and access control mean different things on each track rather than one undifferentiated 'corpus'.

recap.before: The agent drafted from model memory.
recap.after: Drafts are grounded in retrieved authoritative law and matter docs.
recap.newRisk: Grounding alone doesn't guarantee the cited source is current or on-point — it must be verified.

steps.title: 3. A Deterministic Retrieve → Draft → Verify Pipeline
description: Because a wrong citation is a malpractice-grade side effect that must be guarded before the work product is surfaced, the control plane is a deterministic pipeline — retrieve → draft → verify → attorney review — with LLM calls confined to bounded nodes, not an open autonomous loop (the §2 selection rule).

The draft node grounds and cites; the verify node (next step) checks; the pipeline writes the draft. The DraftStore holds the draft through its whole lifecycle, distinguished by status: an unverified intermediate written while the pipeline runs (job_status drafting/verifying/correcting), a verified draft awaiting review (awaiting_review), and the finalized work product after approval — so a draft in flight is never mistaken for one ready for an attorney. Due-diligence work fans across thousands of documents through the same authored path, so scale never bypasses verification.

view.caption: An authored DAG: the model drafts in a bounded node grounded in authoritative law; the pipeline writes a pending draft.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why a fixed pipeline rather than an autonomous research agent that decides its own steps?
patterns: Workflow vs agent (deterministic pipeline vs probabilistic loop)

concepts.definition: Every citation is checked before a draft is ever shown to an attorney, not after.
concepts.whyItMatters: It guards the malpractice-grade side effect at the right point — before the human invests trust in the draft.
concepts.example: A draft with an overruled citation is flagged and corrected before it reaches the review queue.

whyNow: A known, auditable path lets verification sit as a mandatory stage before review.

options.name: Deterministic pipeline (recommended)
pros: Verification is a mandatory, fixed stage.
Predictable and auditable; provenance at each step.
cons: Less flexible for novel research paths.

options.name: Autonomous research loop
cons: Can skip or defer verification; non-deterministic and hard to audit.
Wrong default when a bad citation is malpractice.

recap.before: Drafts could come from an opaque research loop.
recap.after: A deterministic pipeline produces each grounded draft.
recap.newRisk: The draft's citations still must be proven current and on-point before anyone relies on them.

steps.title: 4. The Gate: Verify Every Citation (Existence, Currency, Relevance)
description: This is the crux. A deterministic citation verifier checks every citation on three axes before a draft is surfaced: existence (the source is real), currency (it is still good law — KeyCite flags overruled or questioned authority), and relevance (it actually supports the proposition it's attached to).

Currency is what makes this domain special: a real but overruled citation is worse than a fabricated one, because it reads as authoritative. Any flagged citation sends the draft back through a correction loop, not forward to the attorney — and each attempt writes reviewable evidence: the proposition span, the source span, the KeyCite status, a relevance score, the verifier version, and a decision state (machine-pass, machine-flagged, or attorney-overridden). Existence and currency are deterministic lookups against authority metadata; relevance is a semantic claim, so it is model-assisted with a threshold, and it escalates to the attorney when it sits in the uncertain band rather than silently passing.

An attorney override (§5) never rewrites the machine decision to a pass — the citation stays machine-flagged, and the override is a separate, audited attorney-overridden decision recording that the licensed attorney accepted the risk, with the verification evidence attached.

view.caption: Every citation is checked for existence, currency (KeyCite), and relevance; a flag routes the draft back through the correction loop, not forward to the attorney.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why is a real-but-overruled citation a worse failure than a citation that simply doesn't exist?

concepts.definition: The three checks every citation must pass: it is real, it is still good law, and it supports the stated proposition.
concepts.whyItMatters: Existence alone is not enough — currency catches overruled law and relevance catches off-point cites.
concepts.example: A case exists and is on-point but KeyCite shows it was overruled -> flagged, draft returned.

whyNow: Verification is the first-class component this vertical is built around; it precedes any human review.

deepDives.title: Relevance is not a deterministic lookup
points: Existence and currency resolve against authority metadata (the citation reporter and KeyCite status); they are deterministic.

Relevance — does this authority actually support this proposition — is semantic, so it is model-assisted: score the proposition span against the cited source span, threshold it, and treat an uncertain middle band as a flag, not a pass.
Never let a low-confidence relevance score pass silently; route it to the correction loop or escalate to the attorney with the evidence attached.

deepDives.title: The correction loop and its evidence
points: A flagged citation routes back to the draft node to re-ground and re-cite, then reverifies; each attempt is a row in citation_verifications with verifier_version and attempt number.
Repeated failure on the same proposition stops looping and lands in a human exception queue rather than spinning.
Every attempt and decision appends to the audit record, so a reviewer (or a court) can reconstruct why a cite passed, was corrected, or was overridden.

deepDives.title: Verifying at scale within provider limits
points: KeyCite/Westlaw calls are rate-limited and metered, so cache verification per (citation, source version) and batch lookups; each attempt is a provider_calls row (provider, operation, request_hash, source_version, cache_state, status, retry_count, cost).

Cache freshness for currency is bounded by the corpus freshness_sla: serve a cached KeyCite status within its TTL, but force a refresh before finalization and whenever the pinned source version has advanced — a stale_served status is never allowed to gate an approval.

Wrap provider calls in timeouts and bounded retries with backoff (e.g. 3 attempts) behind a circuit breaker; on provider outage hold the draft in verifying rather than passing it through unverified, and if the breaker stays open past the SLA the draft lands in verification_blocked for human handling.
Spend is bounded beyond the global token budget: a per-matter budget and per-client quota, with interactive memos prioritized over offline diligence batches when the queue is contended.

Pin the source version used for verification so a later publisher update can't silently change a finalized cite's status; repeated correction-loop failure on the same proposition stops retrying and routes to the human exception queue (DLQ) rather than spinning.

recap.after: Every citation is proven real, current, and on-point before review.
recap.newRisk: Even a fully verified draft is not work product until a licensed attorney approves it.

traps.trap: Checking only that a cited case exists.
traps.why: An existing case can be overruled or off-point; existence is the weakest of the three checks.
traps.instead: Verify currency (KeyCite) and relevance too, and return the draft on any flag.

steps.title: 5. The Gate, Part Two: Attorney-in-the-Loop
description: Verification makes a draft trustworthy; it does not make it work product. A licensed attorney must review and approve before anything is finalized or filed — this is mandatory, not optional, and the platform binds approval to a licensed attorney principal via identity.

Review is a workflow, not a single button: assign a reviewer, annotate, request changes (which returns the draft through the correction loop), approve, or reject — and high-risk work can require a second review. Binding finalization to a licensed attorney principal means the platform needs a license/jurisdiction source (a bar-status check or firm-admin attestation), and an attorney override of a flagged citation is allowed only where policy permits and is itself audited. Every action is a durable, ordered review event.
The agent and pipeline are the assistant; the supervising attorney remains responsible (UPL and the professional-conduct rules on supervising non-lawyer/AI assistance). Nothing auto-files.

view.caption: A licensed attorney reviews the verified draft; identity binds approval to the attorney before the work product is finalized.

steps.decisionPrompt: What does the platform check to ensure the approver is a licensed attorney and not the agent itself?
patterns: Delegation not impersonation (OAuth token exchange, act claim)

concepts.definition: A licensed attorney must review and approve AI-assisted work before it becomes finalized or filed work product.
concepts.whyItMatters: Responsibility and the unauthorized-practice line stay with the attorney; the agent cannot be the final authority.
concepts.example: The draft is marked finalized only after the identity broker confirms an attorney principal approved it.

whyNow: The human gate is what makes draft-only autonomy safe for an irreversible, professionally-regulated output.

recap.before: A verified draft could be treated as finished.
recap.after: Only attorney-approved drafts become finalized work product.
recap.newRisk: Client data and findings must be confidential and every finding defensible to a court.

steps.title: 6. Confidentiality & Audit-Defensible Provenance
description: Two non-negotiables sit under everything. Confidentiality: client data runs under zero-data-retention and no-train terms, with tenant segregation and ethical walls enforced — privilege is genuinely at risk otherwise — and matter documents are untrusted input that must be partitioned from other tenants and from tool authority.

Provenance: every finding and sentence traces to the exact source span it came from, so the work is audit-defensible — an attorney (or a court) can follow any assertion back to its source and version.

view.caption: Confidentiality (ZDR/no-train, tenant isolation, ethical walls) plus provenance: every finding traces to a source span.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you make every sentence in a memo defensible back to a source, and keep one client's data away from another's?

concepts.definition: A mapping from each finding to the exact source document, span, and version it was drawn from.
concepts.whyItMatters: Legal work must be defensible; a finding without a traceable source can't be relied on or defended.
concepts.example: A diligence finding links to 'Contract v2, §7.3, lines 4-9'.

whyNow: Confidentiality and provenance are hard requirements that the gate and audit record depend on.

deepDives.title: Why hallucination is treated as first-class here
points: A dated Stanford study reported material hallucination even on grounded legal tools (tied to the model versions tested — not a timeless benchmark).
So verification and provenance are components, not afterthoughts.
Keep raw model traces in a separate, access-controlled store under the same ZDR/no-train terms.

deepDives.title: Retention differs by artifact, and legal hold overrides it
points: These artifacts do not share one policy: prompt traces and retrieved snippets are short-lived and redacted, model outputs and eval samples are kept longer for quality work, finalized work product follows the engagement's retention, and audit events are immutable and retained the longest.
Raw matter documents stay in the DMS under the firm's own retention; the platform holds references and provenance spans, not duplicate copies that outlive the source.

Legal hold overrides deletion: a matter under hold suspends client export/deletion and the normal TTLs, which can conflict with a deletion request — the platform names that boundary (immutable audit and held matters survive deletion; everything else honors export/delete).
Redaction is applied per retention policy on the audit payload itself, so the append-only record stays defensible without re-storing privileged content in the clear.

recap.before: Client data and findings had no isolation or traceability.
recap.after: ZDR/no-train confidentiality with tenant isolation, and every finding traced to a source span.
recap.newRisk: We still need to support the real workflows and measure quality where hallucination is the core failure.

steps.title: 7. Workflows at Scale & Evaluation
description: Support the real legal workflows on this substrate: contract review against a playbook with redline rationale; due-diligence review at scale via tabular extraction across thousands of documents (each cell traced to a source span); and cited research memos.

Evaluate where it matters: against attorney ground truth, tracking citation-error and hallucination rates with trajectory eval, so a fluent-looking output never hides a bad-law citation or an unsupported finding.

view.caption: Contract review, due-diligence tabular extract, and cited memos run on the pipeline; quality is evaluated vs attorney ground truth.

steps.decisionPrompt: A memo reads beautifully but cites one overruled case — which signal catches it, fluency or citation-error eval?

concepts.definition: Measuring how often the system produces non-existent, overruled, off-point, or unsupported citations, against attorney-verified ground truth.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is the domain's defining quality metric; final-answer fluency hides exactly the failure that matters.
concepts.example: Track the rate of flagged citations per memo and unsupported findings per diligence set.

whyNow: Workflows and eval are what let the platform deliver real legal work without losing defensibility.

deepDives.title: Contract review: redlines against a playbook
points: The output is not prose but structured suggestions: each redline ties a playbook_rules rule_id (preferred/fallback position, default severity) to a clause span with a risk severity and a rationale.
The same verify gate applies — any authority cited in a rationale is checked — and the attorney accepts, edits, or rejects each suggested edit.
State lives in the redlines entity against a version-pinned playbook, so a returned contract reuses prior suggestions against the same rule set instead of re-deriving them.

deepDives.title: Due diligence at scale: resumable batch extraction
points: A diligence_batch fans an extraction_schema across thousands of documents; each cell (document × field) is a diligence_finding with a confidence score and a traced source span.

Batches are checkpointed and resumable via diligence_documents per-document status (pending/extracting/done/needs_adjudication/dlq): per-document errors don't fail the batch, docs_done is the resume checkpoint, partial completion is reported, and a poison document lands in the DLQ after its retry budget.
Low-confidence cells route to human adjudication rather than being surfaced as fact; per-matter concurrency caps keep one large batch from starving interactive memo drafting.

deepDives.title: Evaluation as a release gate, not just monitoring
points: Three layers: an offline eval set of attorney-adjudicated cases run before rollout, shadow eval on a sample of live production drafts, and continuous production monitoring of finalized work.

Eval is a release gate: a candidate model, retriever, verifier, prompt, or corpus version ships only if it holds citation-error rate (non-existent/overruled/off-point) and unsupported-finding rate at or below the current baseline on a minimum attorney-adjudicated sample — a regression on either blocks or stages the rollout.

Track metrics by task type (contract_review, diligence, memo) because the failure modes differ — off-point authority in memos, missed playbook positions in redlines, low-confidence cells in diligence.

Stage changes behind a holdout: canary a fraction of traffic, compare against the held-back baseline, and keep the prior model/verifier/corpus version pinned for instant rollback if production citation-error rate drifts.

Trajectory eval inspects the pipeline's intermediate steps, not just the final answer, so a corrected cite or a skipped check is visible; eval traces are retained under the same ZDR/no-train and retention/redaction policy as the rest of the platform.

recap.before: The platform handled one task and quality was unmeasured.
recap.after: Contract review, diligence, and memos run on one verified, attorney-gated pipeline, evaluated against ground truth.
recap.newRisk: This completes phase 1 of the series — Foundations plus three verticals contrasting where the gate sits.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Agentic Legal Platform
finalDesign.description: Matter documents and a request enter through the legal API and queue into a deterministic pipeline (per-matter concurrency caps keep one large diligence batch from starving interactive memo drafting). Retrieval is access-scoped from the first read — tenant, matter, jurisdiction, document version, and ethical walls — so the draft agent only ever grounds in documents the requester may see: authoritative law (Westlaw/Practical Law/KeyCite, version-pinned) and matter docs from the DMS, drafting with citations in a bounded LLM node. A deterministic citation verifier checks every citation for existence, currency (KeyCite), and relevance; a flag routes the draft through a visible correction loop back to the draft node (or, on repeated failure, a human exception queue), and each attempt writes reviewable evidence. The pipeline records provenance for every finding (source span and version) and writes a pending draft. A licensed attorney drives a review workflow — assign, annotate, request changes, approve, or audited cite-override — and the identity broker binds finalization to an attorney principal; nothing auto-files. Confidentiality runs throughout (zero-data-retention, no-train, tenant isolation, ethical walls), every action appends to an immutable, retention-controlled audit record, and the pipeline is evaluated against attorney ground truth on citation-error and hallucination rates. Provider rate limits, outages, and source-version drift are handled with caching, timeouts, retries, and circuit breakers. Retrieval/grounding, eval, and confidentiality are the shared substrate from Agentic Platform Foundations; like the Finance vertical the platform leans deterministic, but the irreversible action it guards is a finalized or filed work product.

functional.how: Playbook-grounded drafting on the deterministic pipeline.
Tabular extraction fanned across documents, each finding traced.
Grounded drafting in authoritative law with citations.
Mandatory attorney-in-the-loop bound to a licensed principal.

nonFunctional.how: Citation verification is a mandatory pipeline stage.
Every finding maps to a source span and version.
Retrieval over Westlaw/Practical Law/KeyCite and matter docs.
Retrieve -> draft -> verify -> review, not a loop.

say: Frame the irreversible action as a finalized/filed work product, with malpractice and privilege at stake.
It builds on Agentic Platform Foundations; like Finance it leans deterministic.
Draw the agent drafting from memory and explain why a real-but-overruled citation is the worst failure.
Ground in authoritative law and the DMS, then a deterministic retrieve -> draft -> verify pipeline.
The citation-verification gate (existence/currency/relevance), the mandatory attorney-in-the-loop, then confidentiality and provenance.
Contract review, due-diligence tabular extract, memos, and eval against attorney ground truth on citation errors.

expectations: Agent drafts grounded in retrieved sources.
Authoritative grounding and a deterministic verify pipeline.
Treats currency (overruled law) as the key citation check, not just existence.
Designs audit-defensible provenance and ZDR/no-train confidentiality.
Picks deterministic control flow via the selection rule; evaluates on citation-error rate; builds on the Foundations substrate.

links.groupDescription: Evidence, citators, and legal-data APIs for treating citation correctness as a gate.
links.title: Hallucinating Law: Legal Mistakes with Large Language Models are Pervasive
links.why: Measures high legal hallucination rates in closed-book LLMs, motivating the baseline warning and the need for grounding plus attorney supervision.

links.groupDescription: Evidence, citators, and legal-data APIs for treating citation correctness as a gate.
links.title: Hallucination-Free? Assessing the Reliability of Leading AI Legal Research Tools
links.why: Evaluates RAG-based commercial legal research tools and shows that retrieval reduces but does not eliminate hallucinations, supporting verify-before-surface.

links.groupDescription: Evidence, citators, and legal-data APIs for treating citation correctness as a gate.
links.title: LegalCiteBench: Evaluating Citation Reliability in Legal Language Models
links.why: Targets citation retrieval, completion, error detection, matching, and verification, which maps directly to the citation verifier and eval harness.

links.groupDescription: Evidence, citators, and legal-data APIs for treating citation correctness as a gate.
links.title: Benchmarking Legal RAG: The Promise and Limits of AI Statutory Surveys
links.why: Shows multi-jurisdictional statutory research failures and retrieval/reasoning error modes, useful for evaluating the citation gate beyond case law.

links.groupDescription: Evidence, citators, and legal-data APIs for treating citation correctness as a gate.
links.title: KeyCite
links.why: Shows the production citator model for checking whether cases, statutes, regulations, or administrative decisions are still good law.

links.groupDescription: Evidence, citators, and legal-data APIs for treating citation correctness as a gate.
links.title: Shepard's Citations Service

links.groupDescription: Evidence, citators, and legal-data APIs for treating citation correctness as a gate.
links.title: CourtListener REST API, v4.4

links.groupDescription: RAG benchmarks and retrieval designs for grounding legal answers in precise source spans.

links.groupDescription: RAG benchmarks and retrieval designs for grounding legal answers in precise source spans.

links.groupDescription: RAG benchmarks and retrieval designs for grounding legal answers in precise source spans.

links.groupDescription: RAG benchmarks and retrieval designs for grounding legal answers in precise source spans.
links.title: Ragas: Automated Evaluation of Retrieval Augmented Generation
links.why: Defines faithfulness, context relevance, and answer relevance metrics that can be adapted to whether a citation supports its proposition.

links.groupDescription: Ethics guidance that turns attorney review from UX preference into a hard workflow requirement.
links.title: Model Rule 1.1 Competence - Comment

links.groupDescription: Ethics guidance that turns attorney review from UX preference into a hard workflow requirement.
links.title: Model Rule 5.3: Responsibilities Regarding Nonlawyer Assistance

links.groupDescription: Ethics guidance that turns attorney review from UX preference into a hard workflow requirement.
